narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and middle-income family background. However, the admissions model predicted a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark used to evaluate applications—leading to the regrettable rejection of yours. This prediction stems from a careful look at how your academic history stacks up against patterns from thousands of past students. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance of others. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lower end versus the average, played a strong supporting role in tipping the scales that way. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, slightly above the dataset average, worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success. Your undergraduate GPA, below the typical 3.21 average, added a small nudge toward the failure side, while your middle family income and full-time student status offered minor positive boosts. Overall, your lower early academic deciles stood out as the most influential drags compared to students who typically pass the bar, outweighing your LSAT edge. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many applicants with similar profiles have improved through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking tutoring or study groups tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Strengthening your GPA through post-bac options could also help on reapplication. We're rooting for you to turn this around and reapply stronger—law schools need diverse voices like yours. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
